summary: Partially covered: RESEARCH.md researched the core stack (Electron, Temporal, Drizzle, tRPC, providers, NotebookLM, GBrain/Hermes) and honestly tags each with a "spike required / remaining risk", but the architecture draft never carries those open items forward into a named Phase-0 spike/gate inventory, and several volatile externals (provider model-IDs/pricing/limits/structured-output fidelity, GBrain SHA pin, NotebookLM Drive-refresh, Granola/connector APIs, Electron signing) are left unresolved without an explicit gate the binding ARCHITECTURE.md can anchor.

findings.finding: The four model providers added beyond the PRD's Claude reference (OpenAI, OpenRouter, Ollama, LM Studio) have NO named Phase-0 conformance spike anywhere, and their model-IDs, pricing, context limits, and structured-output fidelity are unpinned. RESEARCH.md R-005 explicitly demands 'Phase-0 provider conformance must pin exact provider/model pairs', but the PRD §19 spike table contains no provider-matrix spike and the architecture draft only lists 'provider conformance' as a test track, not a gated Phase-0 spike that pins these volatile values.
findings.evidence: RESEARCH.md R-005 remaining risk (lines 137-138); OQ-003/OQ-004 (OPEN_QUESTIONS.md lines 11-12); PRD §19 spike table (system_of_work_assistant_prd_v0_3.md lines 1062-1075 — no provider-matrix spike); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §7 (lines 214-235) + §12 line 322 (test only)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a named 'Model Provider Conformance' Phase-0 spike to the binding ARCHITECTURE.md spike inventory: pin exact provider/model pairs per capability, record pricing/context-limit/tool-call/structured-output-fidelity results, and feed a config-held provider matrix (resolving OQ-003/OQ-004 defaults). Name the no-go branch: disable the failing provider/capability pair, Claude SDK critical path unaffected.
findings.anchor: §7 Provider and Runtime Broker / new Phase-0 Spike inventory

findings.finding: Structured-output fidelity varies across the locked provider set (Claude tool-use-based, OpenAI native strict json_schema, OpenRouter passthrough of heterogeneous backends, Ollama/LM Studio model-dependent), yet the architecture's load-bearing 'strict JSON Schema gate before side effects' assumes providers can emit schema-valid output and names no repair/retry strategy for providers that cannot guarantee it. This is an unresearched external-behavior dependency, not just an internal design choice.
findings.evidence: RESEARCH.md R-005 (line 138, 'structured output fidelity... volatile'); DECISIONS.md ADR-004 (lines 77-85, strict schema gate); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §7 strict side-effect rule (lines 232-235)
findings.proposed_fix: In §7, require the provider conformance spike to measure per-provider structured-output fidelity and specify the broker's behavior when native strict-schema output is unavailable (e.g., schema-prompt + validate + bounded repair-retry, else fail the job) rather than assuming uniform schema compliance.

findings.finding: The committed V1 NotebookLM path (Drive-backed managed docs) depends on an unvalidated assumption about NotebookLM's Drive-source refresh behavior — i.e. whether NotebookLM re-ingests updated managed Drive docs automatically or requires manual user re-import (the PRD already corrected an earlier 'auto-sync' claim). The §19 'NotebookLM API' spike only covers the DEFERRED direct-API CRUD path, so the actually-shipping path has no validating spike.
findings.evidence: RESEARCH.md R-006 remaining risk (line 159, 'Drive doc refresh behavior and user workflow must be tested'); PRD §19 NotebookLM API spike (line 1068, direct-API only); PRD line 29 (auto-sync claim corrected); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §8 Notebook (lines 250-253)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a Phase-0 'NotebookLM Drive-refresh' validation distinct from the direct-API spike: confirm whether managed-Drive-doc updates surface in NotebookLM without manual re-import, and document the resulting user workflow/limits in §8. Flag the refresh behavior as the V1 risk, not the deferred API.

findings.finding: External SaaS connector APIs are entirely absent from RESEARCH.md — there is no research entry for Granola, Calendar, Todoist, Linear, Asana, Drive/Docs, GitHub, or Telegram (rate limits, scopes, MCP availability, endpoint behavior). Granola in particular feeds the locked P0 meeting-closeout proof spine and has its own PRD §19 spike, yet it is unresearched and the architecture draft §8 lists connectors without naming the Granola connector spike.
findings.evidence: RESEARCH.md covers only R-001..R-007 (no connector entry); PRD §19 'Granola Connector' spike (line 1069); PRD §22.1 (line 1196, 'Vendor APIs for Granola, Calendar... should be checked'); meeting-closeout locked as proof spine (DECISIONS.md line 11); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §8 (lines 239-247)
findings.proposed_fix: Name a Phase-0 Granola connector spike (transcript/metadata/decisions/actions pull + calendar correlation) in §8 since it gates the P0 proof spine, and add a tracked 'connector API revalidation' item (rate limits/scopes/MCP-vs-REST) for the remaining connectors per PRD §22.1.

findings.finding: The PRD requires a pinned GBrain known-good version with the SHA recorded in repo config and a compatibility/contract gate, but the architecture draft's GBrain rules and deployment sections never mention version pinning or the contract gate — only write-through containment.
findings.evidence: PRD §18.1 Phase 0 (line 1023, 'GBrain known-good version selected and pinned (SHA recorded in repo config)') and Phase 2 (line 1025, 'GBrain compatibility contract test green against the pinned version'); OQ-006 (line 14); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §6 GBrain rules (lines 202-206) and §13 (lines 341-342) omit the pin/contract gate
findings.proposed_fix: Add to §6/§13: pin a GBrain commit/tag (SHA in repo config) and gate GBrain-dependent phases on a GBrain compatibility contract test against that pinned version. The exact SHA is a Phase-0 GBrain-spike output (carry OQ-006 as a question-for-human until the spike runs).

findings.finding: The Hermes integration surface is unresolved — API server vs TUI gateway vs Python wrapper vs Kanban vs hybrid — and the architecture draft names the Hermes adapter without naming the surface-selection spike or its no-go branch. This is a load-bearing external-integration decision the owner/spike must make; do not assume a surface.
findings.evidence: OQ-007 (OPEN_QUESTIONS.md line 15); PRD §19 'Hermes Adapter Surface' spike (line 1064); RESEARCH.md R-007 (lines 178-182); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §7 (line 230) names adapter only
findings.proposed_fix: Carry OQ-007 as a question-for-human and name the 'Hermes Adapter Surface' Phase-0 spike in §7 with its parallel-track no-go branch (Claude SDK remains critical path; Hermes required+DoD-tested but not first-install gating per PRD §20.1).

findings.finding: Electron signing/notarization, hardened-runtime entitlements, and packaging of the spawned Node worker + Temporal dev-server + GBrain subprocesses are unresolved and absent from the architecture draft's deployment section. For a production-grade build this is load-bearing: hardened runtime / Gatekeeper / library-validation can block subprocess spawning on other Macs, directly affecting the open-source clean-install reproducibility DoD.
findings.evidence: OQ-001 (line 9); RESEARCH.md R-001 remaining risk (line 42, 'Packaging/supervision/signing details require Phase-0 shell spike'); PRD §13.3 (line 410, 'signed/notarized builds later if useful'); install reproducibility metric/DoD (PRD lines 211, 1122); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §13 (lines 335-344) silent on signing
findings.proposed_fix: Carry OQ-001 as a question-for-human (signed+notarized vs documented unsigned local build for V1) and name a Phase-0 Electron shell/packaging spike in §13 that validates code-signing/notarization + hardened-runtime entitlements against spawning the worker/Temporal/GBrain subprocesses, tied to the clean-install acceptance test.

findings.finding: The macOS wake-hook mechanism that triggers Temporal schedule catch-up and in-flight resume is named as a remaining risk in research but the architecture draft says 'resume on wake' without naming the detection mechanism (e.g., NSWorkspace didWake / power-management notifications) that the LIFE-1..6 requirements depend on.
findings.evidence: RESEARCH.md R-002 remaining risk (line 66, 'OS wake hooks require implementation validation'); PRD §19 Sleep/Wake Lifecycle spike (line 1072) + §21 LIFE-1..6 (line 1147); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §9 (lines 260-262)
findings.proposed_fix: In §9/§13, name the macOS wake-detection mechanism the worker uses (power/NSWorkspace wake notification) feeding Temporal catch-up + outbox replay, and reference the existing Sleep/Wake Lifecycle spike as its validation gate.

findings.finding: The architecture draft has no consolidated Phase-0 external-dependency spike/gate section; it implicitly defers to PRD §18.1/§19 but never restates the spikes, their go/no-go criteria, or their named fallback branches. Since every RESEARCH.md finding ends in 'spike required'/'remaining risk', the binding ARCHITECTURE.md needs one place that enumerates these external-dependency gates and what each no-go triggers.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md has §13 deploy, §15 scope, §16 audit targets but no spike inventory; RESEARCH.md R-001..R-007 all end with spike/remaining-risk lines; PRD §18.1 Phase 0 (line 1023) and §19 (lines 1060-1075) hold the canonical spike list
findings.proposed_fix: Add a 'Phase-0 Spikes & External-Dependency Gates' section to the binding ARCHITECTURE.md restating each spike (GBrain Round Trip, GBrain Full Capability Bridge, Provider Conformance, Hermes Adapter Surface, NotebookLM API + Drive-refresh, Granola Connector, Sleep/Wake Lifecycle, Electron packaging/signing) with go/no-go and the fallback branch each no-go selects, classified critical-path vs parallel vs non-blocking.
